title: Guinea-Bissau pilots a new model for early grade teaching
gist: A World Bank-financed structured-pedagogy pilot across 25 schools in Guinea-Bissau's Oio region — scripted lesson plans, active-learning teacher training, regular coaching, and 1:1 learning materials — targets a severe early-grade literacy crisis (only 17% of 7-14 year-olds could read a basic Portuguese text) and is designed as a proof-of-concept for national scale-up, echoing a similar national rollout already underway in the Gambia with the same NGO partner.

insights.claim: A MICS 2018-19 learning assessment of Guinea-Bissau children aged 7-14 found only 17% could correctly read 90% of a basic text in Portuguese, and fewer than 1 in 10 had basic numeracy skills.
insights.speaker: @worldbank
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of Guinea-Bissau's learning crisis — a baseline this low means even modest gains from structured pedagogy could be high-leverage for human-capital-linked development financing.
insights.quote: only 17 percent could correctly read 90 percent of a basic text in Portuguese, and fewer than 1 in 10 had basic numeracy skills

insights.claim: National classroom observation in Guinea-Bissau consistently found teachers arriving without lesson plans, relying almost entirely on oral instruction, and having little access to coaching or feedback.
insights.speaker: @worldbank
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific instructional gaps (no lesson plans, no coaching) that the pilot is designed to close — a checklist for evaluating whether similar interventions elsewhere target the right levers.
insights.quote: many teachers arrive without lesson plans, rely almost entirely on oral instruction, and have little access to coaching or feedback

insights.claim: Structured pedagogy, as defined by the What Works Hub, is a teaching model built on a well-sequenced competency progression paired with detailed lesson plans, student learning materials, introductory teacher training, and ongoing coaching or supervision.
insights.speaker: @worldbank
insights.so_what: A reusable, evidence-based instructional model — a relevant benchmark for evaluating any early-grade literacy program across low-income countries, not just Guinea-Bissau.
insights.quote: a teaching and learning approach centered on a well-sequenced and scoped progression of competencies for children to master and an aligned package of detailed lesson plans, student learning materials, introductory teacher training, ongoing coaching and/or supervision for teachers

insights.claim: The pilot covers 25 schools in Guinea-Bissau's Oio region under the World Bank-financed Guinea-Bissau Human Capital Project, delivered with NGO partner Effective Intervention; it began at the preschool level and adds one grade per year, reaching grade 2 by 2028.
insights.speaker: @worldbank
insights.so_what: Defines the pilot's scale and timeline — a slow, staged grade-by-grade rollout through 2028 means measurable system-wide impact will be multi-year, not immediate.
insights.locator: The pilot, which began at the preschool level

insights.claim: The pilot provides learning materials, including the Vamos Aprender textbook aligned with the World Bank PRIEQT curricular reform, to students in a 1:1 ratio, and caps class sizes at 30 students per cohort.
insights.speaker: @worldbank
insights.so_what: Concrete input-side commitments (1:1 materials, capped class size) make the pilot comparable to other input-heavy literacy interventions when assessing cost-effectiveness.

insights.claim: Coaches visit pilot classrooms regularly to observe lessons and give feedback, and annual start- and end-of-year assessments measure whether student learning is actually improving, creating an accountability loop meant to hold the initiative to real learning gains rather than just completed activities.
insights.speaker: @worldbank
insights.so_what: The coaching-plus-measurement loop is the mechanism meant to convert teacher training into actual learning gains; its presence is the key diligence point for judging whether this pilot will outperform training-only programs.
insights.quote: holding the initiative accountable for real changes in learning, not just for activities completed
insights.locator: To make sure the training sticks

insights.claim: The Oio pilot's stated long-term ambition is not to improve outcomes only in its 25 schools, but to build the evidence and systems needed to make structured pedagogy standard practice in every classroom in Guinea-Bissau.
insights.speaker: @worldbank
insights.so_what: Signals this is a proof-of-concept for a national program — success should be judged against system-scaling potential, not just the pilot's own 25-school outcomes.
insights.quote: The longer-term ambition is not simply to improve outcomes in 25 schools in the north of the country. It is to build the evidence and systems needed to make these fundamentals standard across every school in Guinea-Bissau
insights.locator: The Oio pilot is, in part, a response

insights.claim: The same NGO partner, Effective Intervention, is already scaling structured pedagogy nationally in the Gambia under the World Bank-financed Gambia RISE Project, giving the Oio pilot a regional precedent for its own scale-up path.
insights.speaker: @worldbank
insights.so_what: The Gambia's national rollout with the same implementation partner is the closest comparable case to watch for evidence on whether Guinea-Bissau's pilot can scale similarly.
insights.locator: Structured pedagogy pilots are not new in the region
